title: Cauliflower Cheese
subtitle: Florets in a thick bechamel, bubbled under the grill until golden
excerpt: Cauliflower cheese is a baked bechamel dish: the cauliflower is par-boiled, arranged in a dish, covered in a thick cheese sauce, and finished under a hot grill or in a high oven until the top bubbles and colours. The sauce needs to be well seasoned and thick enough to coat the florets without running off. A sharp Cheddar gives the best flavour; a combination of Cheddar and a little Parmesan gives both sharpness and a good top crust.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: cauliflower au gratin with a cheese sauce. Delia Smith, Complete Cookery Course (1978): cauliflower cheese method. Classic British supper and Sunday roast side dish.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Par-boil the cauliflower and make the sauce up to 2 hours ahead. Assemble, cover, and refrigerate. Add 10 minutes to the baking time if going in from cold.

glossaryTerms.definition: A white sauce made from a butter-and-flour roux cooked out with milk. The ratio of flour to butter to milk determines the thickness: a coating sauce for cauliflower cheese uses more flour than a pouring sauce for pasta.

content.text: The key to good cauliflower cheese is a thick

: thin sauce runs off the florets and pools in the bottom of the dish, leaving the cauliflower uncoated. The sauce should hold its shape on a spoon. Season it assertively; cauliflower is mild and needs the sauce to carry the flavour.

Heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan). Bring a large saucepan of salted water to the boil. Add the cauliflower florets and cook for 5 to 6 minutes until just tender but with some resistance at the core. Drain in a colander and leave to steam-dry for 5 minutes.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in the saucepan over a medium heat. Add {{plain-flour}} of flour and stir constantly for 2 minutes to cook out the raw flour taste. Remove from the heat and gradually add {{whole-milk}} of warm milk, whisking after each addition to keep the sauce smooth. Return to the heat, stirring until the sauce thickens and bubbles for 2 minutes. Remove from the heat. Stir in most of the {{cheddar}} of Cheddar, the {{mustard-powder}}, {{sea-salt-flakes}}, and {{black-pepper}}. Taste and adjust seasoning.

Arrange the drained cauliflower in a single layer in a baking dish. Pour the cheese sauce evenly over the top. Scatter the remaining Cheddar and the {{parmesan}} of Parmesan if using. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until the top is golden-brown and bubbling at the edges. Rest for 5 minutes before serving.

rows.symptom: Sauce thin and watery after baking
rows.cause: Cauliflower released too much water during baking
rows.fix: Steam-dry the par-boiled cauliflower thoroughly before adding the sauce. Make the sauce slightly thicker than seems necessary: it will loosen as the cauliflower releases moisture.

rows.cause: Oven not hot enough or dish too deep
rows.fix: Switch to the grill for the final 5 minutes if the top is not colouring. Watch constantly under the grill.

rows.cause: Milk added to hot roux too quickly
rows.fix: Add the milk off the heat in small additions, whisking after each. If already lumpy, strain through a fine sieve.

content.text: Cauliflower cheese appears on the Sunday roast table as a side dish in the same pan it was baked in, brought to the table alongside the joint. It is also a substantial supper dish in its own right, served with good bread. The version with a mustard note in the sauce is the standard British home cook's recipe; some versions add a little cayenne, others a grating of nutmeg.
